title: SEGMENT 3 — Genes that drive cancer: gas pedals and brakes

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — Genes that drive cancer: gas pedals and brakes
How does a cell actually acquire those hallmarks?
The short answer is: by accumulating mutations in particular genes.
Not random genes — specific, identifiable genes whose normal jobs are to regulate growth, repair DNA, or control cell death.
Mess with those genes, and the cell starts moving down the road toward cancer.
And here's where I want to give you the single most useful conceptual distinction in all of cancer genetics.
There are two big categories of cancer-driving genes, and they fail in completely different ways.
Imagine a car.
To drive a car safely you need two things to work: you need the gas pedal to do what you tell it to, and you need the brakes to work when you press them.
If something goes wrong with the gas pedal — say it gets stuck down to the floor — the car accelerates uncontrollably.
If something goes wrong with the brakes — say they fail — the car can't stop when it needs to.
Both failures lead to a crash.
But they're very different failures, mechanically.
That's exactly the situation inside a cell.
Oncogenes are the gas pedals.
In their normal form they're called proto-oncogenes, and they're genes that tell cells to grow and divide.
Their job is healthy and necessary — without them, you'd never heal a wound, never form an embryo.
But when a proto-oncogene gets mutated in the wrong way, it becomes hyperactive — stuck in the "on" position — and the cell receives a relentless internal command to divide.
The pedal is jammed to the floor.
Tumor suppressor genes are the brakes.
Their job is to stop cell division when something is wrong: when DNA is damaged, when the cell is too crowded, when the conditions aren't right.
When tumor suppressor genes get mutated, they fail to do their stopping job.
The brakes are broken.
The cell can't slow down even when it should.
Here's the asymmetry that matters.
To jam a gas pedal, you only need to break one gas pedal.
You have two copies of every gene — one from your mother, one from your father — but one mutated copy of a proto-oncogene that gets stuck "on" is enough to drive the cell forward.
The other normal copy can't undo the damage; it's still happily doing its job, but its job is just to occasionally press the pedal, not to release a stuck one.
We say oncogene mutations are dominant — one bad copy is enough.
But brakes are different.
If you have two sets of brakes on a car — front and rear — losing the front brakes is a problem, but you can still stop with the rear.
You only crash when both sets fail.
That's exactly how tumor suppressor genes work.
You're born with two functional copies.
Lose one copy, and the cell is fine — the other copy picks up the slack.
Lose both copies, and the brakes are gone entirely.
Tumor suppressor mutations are recessive at the cellular level — you need to lose both copies to lose the function.
This insight has a name: it's called Knudson's two-hit hypothesis, after Alfred Knudson, who worked it out in 1971 from studying a childhood eye cancer called retinoblastoma.
He noticed that some kids got the disease in both eyes very young, and some got it in only one eye, later.

The two-eyed kids, he reasoned, must have inherited one already-broken copy of a tumor suppressor gene from a parent — so they only needed one more mutation in any single cell to lose both copies and develop a tumor.
The one-eyed kids started with two good copies and needed two unlikely mutations in the same cell, which is why their cancer was rarer and later.
He was right.
The gene he predicted, RB1, was later cloned and shown to be exactly the tumor suppressor he had described.
So that's the central distinction.
Carry it with you for the whole lecture.
Oncogenes are gas pedals stuck on; tumor suppressors are broken brakes.
Both lead to a crash, but you fix them differently.
You can design a drug to inhibit a stuck gas pedal — block it, calm it down, restore normal behavior.
But you can't, with a small molecule, easily restore a missing brake.
You can't drug an absence.
That asymmetry is going to shape almost everything about which cancers we can treat and how.
